header: Head of B2B Unit / Quinto Andar

lesson.menu_desc: Persuasive conditionals & frames: turn a summary into a winning proposal -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 18 -- The Proposal -- Persuasive Business Writing
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eduardo Chiba | Aula 18 -- The Proposal

listenings.text: Hi Eduardo, it is Sarah. Last week you wrote the executive summary, and it was clear. Now I need the next step: turn that summary into a proposal that actually wins the partner over. A summary just informs; a proposal has to persuade. So lead with the rationale, the reason this matters to them, not to us. Make the benefit compelling, and make it about their upside. Then use a persuasive if: if they approve the pilot, we will onboard their team within thirty days. Add an incentive, something that makes saying yes easy, like a reduced fee for the first quarter. Keep it viable, nothing we cannot deliver. Close by asking them to endorse the next step. Remember, the partner does not want a report. He wants a reason to say yes.

Let me think about how to persuade before I write a single line. A weak proposal lists what we do. A strong one gives the reader a reason to act. So I start with the rationale: why this matters to them. Then I make the benefit compelling and concrete, framed as their upside, not our revenue. The persuasive part is the conditional. If you approve this pilot, we will deliver the first results in thirty days. Should you decide to proceed, we will assign a dedicated team. Each if opens a door and shows what is on the other side. I add one incentive to lower the barrier, I keep every promise viable, and I ask the partner to endorse the plan. A proposal is not a description. It is an argument designed to earn a yes.

extra_audio.text: First, you open with the rationale, the reason the proposal matters to the reader. Then you make the benefit compelling and frame it as their upside. After that, you use a persuasive if: if you approve this, we will deliver. Next, you add one incentive that makes saying yes easy. Finally, you ask the reader to endorse the next step.
